title: Existence of the Wealth-Consumption Ratio in Asset Pricing Models with Recursive Preferences
abstract: Modern asset pricing models combine recursive preferences with complex dynamics for the underlying consumption process. The existence of solutions is for many of these models an unsettled question. This paper introduces a novel technique to prove existence and nonexistence, as well as uniqueness for models with recursive preferences. The approach applies to many models of interest, including those with long-run consumption risks, with stochastic volatility and jumps, with time-varying consumption disasters, and with smooth ambiguity aversion and learning. Collectively, the proven results settle the existence question for many of today’s leading asset pricing models.

author.Ole Wilms*: Universität Hamburg, Germany and Tilburg University,the Netherlands

Recursive formulations of utility have taken center stage in financial economics. Such formulations have allowed for the incorporation of subtle psychological effects, such as a preference for the early resolution of risk (Kreps and Porteus 1978;Epstein and Zin 1989;Weil 1990) or ambiguity aversion (Gilboa and Schmeidler 1989;Hansen and Sargent 2001;Klibanoff, Marinacci, and Mukerji 2009). Combined with very persistent state processes or with stochastic volatility or jumps, these utility functions have shown promise in explaining major asset pricing puzzles, such as the equity premium, excess volatility, or the variance risk premium (see, among others,Bansal and Yaron 2004;Drechsler and Yaron 2011;Wachter 2013;Ju and Miao 2012). Surprisingly, the question of existence—that is, whether or not lifetime utility is well defined—and the uniqueness of these models is not a settled problem.

We introduce a framework to address the existence and uniqueness question for recursive utility and persistent consumption processes with jumps in endowment economies. The existence question is surprisingly delicate: very small and inconspicuous changes in the consumption process can destroy existence. This observation provides a partial explanation for why the problem has resisted resolution for so long. Our approach provides an interesting contrast to the traditional approach of contraction mappings on metric spaces in that it relies on order properties of the utility problem rather than metric properties. We show that our existence results pick out a specific unique solution as the limit of finite-horizon solutions. It is the smallest nonnegative solution, one that can be viewed as the result of value-function iteration starting at zero.

As an illustration of the approach, consider the case of Epstein-Zin utility (seeEpstein and Zin 1989;Weil 1990), where preferences are determined by the degree of risk aversion of the agent and the intertemporal elasticity of substitution (IES). Epstein-Zin utility can be expressed as a function of the wealth-consumption ratio. The existence problem is then equivalent to proving that the wealth-consumption ratio remains finite. We provide two bounds for this quantity. We call the first bound the Minkowski bound as for cases of interest it follows from the Minkowski inequality in functional analysis. Similarly, the second bound follows from the Jensen inequality in functional analysis, so we call it the Jensen bound. Together the two bounds provide an upper and a lower bound for the wealth-consumption ratio. Interestingly, the role of each bound depends on the agent’s preference parameters: if the EIS is greater than one, then the Jensen is the upper bound and the Minkowski bound is the lower bound, while if the EIS is less than one, the roles are reversed. Together, the two bounds can be used to show existence as well as nonexistence for Epstein-Zin utility.

The beauty of our approach is that for models in the discrete-time affine class (seeDuffie, Pan, and Singleton 2000;Eraker 2008), which include the majority of recent macro-finance papers, we can obtain the conditions for existence and nonexistence in closed form.1More precisely, we can show analytically whether or not the two bounds are finite. Showing that the lower bound is infinite is sufficient for proving nonexistence, while showing that the upper bound is finite is sufficient for proving existence. The conditions yield clear interpretations in terms of the preference parameters as well as the parameters of the underlying consumption process. This allows us to directly determine the effects of, for example, jumps, persistent consumption risks, or stochastic volatility on the existence of a solution.

But our approach is not limited to affine processes and Epstein-Zin preferences. It is sufficiently general to cover other recursive preference specifications, such as multiple prior preferences, multiplier preferences, and smooth ambiguity aversion (Gilboa and Schmeidler 1989;Hansen and Sargent 2001;Maccheroni, Marinacci, and Rustichini 2006;Klibanoff, Marinacci, and Mukerji 2009), as well as models with Bayesian learning and regime-switching processes. We demonstrate how to derive closed-form conditions of existence and nonexistence in the models ofCecchetti, Lam, and Mark (1990),Collin-Dufresne, Johannes, and Lochstoer (2016), andJu and Miao (2012)as examples.

Our bounds have three important properties. First, to derive our theoretical results we do not need to truncate the state space. Most asset pricing models include Gaussian shocks and hence are unbounded. Recently,Borovička and Stachurski (2020)have proposed an approach to proving existence and uniqueness for models with Epstein-Zin preferences. However, as they assume a bounded state space, many recent asset pricing models are not covered by this approach. Our approach can handle unbounded state spaces and hence can be used to prove existence and nonexistence in many prominent asset pricing models.

Second, our existence theorems are not limited to Gaussian shocks but are sufficiently general so that they allow for tail risks, such as jumps in consumption growth, time varying mean growth rates, or stochastic volatility. These features have been shown to be particularly important for explaining asset pricing puzzles (see, e.g.,Drechsler and Yaron 2011;Wachter 2013) but up to now the effect of these risks on the existence of a solution have been ambiguous. We provide closed-form conditions that can settle the existence question for models featuring such risks.

Third, as we derive closed-form solutions for our existence conditions they allow for a clear economic interpretation in terms of the preference parameters as well as the parameters driving the consumption process. We show that the magnitude of the time discount factor and of the IES are crucial for the existence of a solution. Increasing the time discount factor increases the wealth-consumption ratio and hence it becomes more likely that a solution no longer exists. For an IES larger than one, the substitution effect dominates the wealth effect. So as long as the mean growth rate is positive, increasing the IES will make the existence condition more stringent. Put differently, a large IES makes the existence of a solution less likely. These two effects are the same in any of the models considered in this paper. Furthermore, we show that any source of risk adds another term to the existence condition. For example, if the volatility of consumption is stochastic or if persistent shocks to the mean growth rate of consumption are included, we obtain an additional term that makes the Jensen bound more stringent. Hence, for each of the models we can separately analyze how different sources of risk affect the existence of a solution.

As we provide two separate bounds for existence and for nonexistence, it is reasonable to ask how tight these bounds are. To answer this question, we first show, in a well-known example—namely, the long-run risk model ofBansal and Yaron (2004)—that the bounds are very tight for reasonable parameter values. We also include jumps in the mean growth rate to show that the bounds do not become loose once tail risks are included. Furthermore, we settle the question of existence for a large number of prominent asset pricing papers that have proposed different sources of risk to explain asset pricing puzzles. These include models with consumption disasters as in the work ofWachter (2013), models with jumps in volatility as in the work ofDrechsler and Yaron (2011), and models with Bayesian learning as in the work ofCollin-Dufresne, Johannes, and Lochstoer (2016), and smooth ambiguity aversion as in the work ofJu and Miao (2012). We show that for all the calibrations used in these models the wealth-consumption ratio is finite, and hence that a solution exists.2

Our approach is complementary to the existing approaches in the literature. The traditional recipe for proving existence for infinite-horizon models, going back toStokey and Lucas (1989), is to reformulate the existence problem as a fixed-point equation for a contraction mapping in a metric space. This approach relies on the choice of the metric to prove existence.Epstein and Zin (1989)originally proved existence for their utility function by using this approach, adapting the contraction theorem ofBoyd III (1990).Marinacci and Montrucchio (2010)extend this to some growth economies by a clever choice of metric. In contrast, we exploit the fact that most recursive utilities used in practice are homogeneous, which allows them to be reformulated in terms of the wealth-consumption ratio. Our bounds on this ratio are sufficient to imply existence.

Parallel to our paper,Borovička and Stachurski (2020)have developed an alternate approach, based on a local spectral radius theorem, showing existence in asset pricing models. The condition ofBorovička and Stachurski (2020)has the attractive property of being necessary as well as sufficient. However, its disadvantage is that it requires the assumption of a bounded state space and its focus on (truncated) Gaussian shocks. A second disadvantage is that the existence condition ofBorovička and Stachurski (2020)generally needs to be computed numerically, for example, by Monte Carlo simulations. Using a stylized example for which a closed-form solution exists, we show inInternet Appendix Bthat the numerical approach is problematic for highly persistent state processes as used in many recent asset pricing models.Hansen and Scheinkman (2012)pioneered the technique of using spectral methods to prove existence.Borovička and Stachurski (2021)use a similar approach to prove the existence and uniqueness of the prices of risky assets andStachurski, Zhang, and Wilms (2022)analyze the impact of preference shocks on the existence and uniqueness of recursive utilities.

Another approach, and the one closest to ours, is to use order-theoretic methods.Kamihigashi, Reffett, and Yao (2015)provide an early example.Becker and Rincon-Zapatero (2017)reformulate the approach ofMarinacci and Montrucchio (2010)in order-theoretic terms.Christensen (2022)uses a mixture of order-theoretic and spectral methods to prove existence and uniqueness, particularly for the case IES=1. The key step for this argument is a thin-tail condition on growth rates. In contrast, we exploit the fact that most models in the literature fall into the discrete-time affine process family, which allows us to give explicit bounds that are easy to check.
In this section, we present our results on existence, nonexistence, and uniqueness in asset pricing models with a representative agent and recursive preferences. Section 1.1 presents our existence and nonexistence theorems and we discuss uniqueness in Section 1.2. The results are sufficiently general to cover a broad class of recursive utility formulations and in Section 1.3 we show how the theorems specialize in the case of Epstein-Zin preferences (seeEpstein and Zin 1989;Weil 1990). Our results can be directly applied to a broad class of consumption processes that fall into the affine class, which we show in Section 1.4. Sections 1.1 and 1.2 are kept at the most general level, so the reader who is mainly interested in applications to asset pricing models can go directly to Section 1.3.

1.1 Existence and nonexistence for recursive equations

In this section we present abstract general results, which we later specialize to specific models. We are interested in proving existence for recursive equations of the form v t = S t v t + 1 , (1) for a sequence of random variables ( v t ) t = 0 ∞ ⁠ . We say that Equation (1) has a solution if the generated sequence ( v t ) is well defined and remains finite (for all t ). Here S t is an operator that turns time- ( t + 1 ) random variables into time- t random variables. For most of our applications to specific asset pricing models this will be applied to homogeneous utility specifications, or a normalized form of them (where they can be interpreted as the wealth-consumption ratio), but this is not required for the results of this section.

A simple example of such anStcan be found in the recursive equation for the wealth-consumption ratio under constant relative-risk aversion (CRRA) utility. In this case,δαStvt+1=1+δEt(eαΔct+1vt+1)⁠, whereδandαare parameters, andΔct+1is the log growth rate of consumption. The operatorStis required to satisfy one technical condition for the existence results, which we call the Monotone Convergence Property (MCP); seeAppendix Afor a precise statement. Any operator built around expectations, such as those in Epstein-Zin or smooth ambiguity-aversion preferences, satisfies the property.

In general, the recursive system (1) can have infinitely many solutions. The solution we consider in this paper is the limit of finite-horizon solutions. We show in Section 1.2 that this is the unique homogeneous solution that does not contain a bubble. We define the finite-horizon solution v t , T for time horizon T as follows: v t , T = S t v t + 1 , T for t < T and v T , T = S T 0 ⁠ . The limit solution for the infinite-horizon economy is then the limit at T → ∞ ⁠ , v t = lim T → ∞ v t , T . (2)

We prove two types of existence results, which we call the “Jensen” and the “Minkowski” existence theorems, as they follow from Jensen’s inequality and from the Minkowski inequality in functional analysis, respectively. Jensen’s existence theorem is a comparison result: it uses existence (or nonexistence) for one operator to conclude existence (or nonexistence) for another operator. The Minkowski existence theorem is a sufficient condition to conclude existence or nonexistence. By combining the two types of results we can establish existence results that cover a large swath of the parameter space considered in asset pricing models.

First we describe the Minkowski result, which requires exploiting specific features of the operator. Suppose S t can be written as S t x t + 1 = G t + H t x t + 1 , for G t a time- t random variable, and H t an operator. (In our applications, G t = 1 but this is inessential.) For example, for the special case of the wealth-consumption ratio under CRRA utility, H t x t + 1 is δ α δ E t ( e α Δ c t + 1 x t + 1 ) ⁠ . The operator H t is superadditive if H t ( x t + 1 + y t + 1 ) ≥ H t x t + 1 + H t y t + 1 , (3) and subadditive if H t ( x t + 1 + y t + 1 ) ≤ H t x t + 1 + H t y t + 1 . (4)

Note thatHtis both superadditive and subadditive exactly when it is additive. Using properties ofHt, we can express a bound onvtthat can be used to settle many cases of existence or nonexistence. The Minkowski bound splits into two cases, based on whetherHtis superadditive or subadditive.

The intuition behind the bound is simple. For example, supposeHtis superadditive, and for simplicity assumeGt= 1. Thenvt=1+Htvt+1=1+Ht(1+Ht+1vt+2)⁠. By superadditivity,Ht(1+Ht+1vt+2)≥1+HtHt+1vt+2⁠. This allows us to boundvtby1+Ht1+HtHt+1vt+2⁠. We can repeat this process forvt+2and so on to give a bound entirely in terms of iterated application ofHtto one. In the limit,vt≥1+Ht1+HtHt+11+⋯⁠. This is the Minkowski bound in this special case. The following theorem states the general case.

Theorem 1 (General Minkowski Bound). Let H t be an operator that takes time- ( t + 1 ) random variables to time- t random variables and satisfies the MCP. Let g t , T be defined by g T , T = G T ⁠ , and g t , T = H t g t + 1 , T for t < T . Define w t to be w t = ∑ i ∈ N 0 g t , t + i . (5) If H t is subadditive, then the limit solution v t ≤ w t ⁠ . If H t is superadditive, then v t ≥ w t ⁠ . (Thus, if H t is additive, v t = w t .) Proof. See Appendix A . ■

IfHttakes nonnegative random variables to nonnegative random variables (as it does in all of our applications), thenwtis always well defined, though it may be infinite. Hence, ifHtis subadditive, a sufficient condition for existence ofvtis to show thatwtis finite. In contrast, ifHtis superadditive, a sufficient condition for nonexistence ofvtis to show thatwtis infinite. The bound is most useful for those models that permit a closed-form expression forgt,t+i⁠. In Section 1.4 we show how to obtaingt,t+ifor a broad class of asset pricing models.

The Minkowski bound gives us either an upper or a lower bound for a parameterized model. We now state a second existence result that allows us to compare existence results across models. We call it the Jensen bound as it follows from Jensen’s inequality. As we argue below, for many models a natural point of comparison is with CRRA utility, since in that case combining the Minkowski bound and the Jensen bound yields both a lower and an upper bound for each model.

Theorem 2 (General Jensen Bound). Let S t and R t be two operators that take time- ( t + 1 ) nonnegative random variables to time- t random variables, such that for 0 ≤ x t + 1 ≤ y t + 1 we have 0 ≤ S t x t + 1 ≤ S t y t + 1 ≤ R t y t + 1 . (6) Then if R t has a nonnegative solution, so does S t . The solution for S t is the limit of finite-horizon solutions. Proof. See Appendix A . ■

Theorem 2 allows us to make relative statements about the solution of recursive equations, that is, about continuation values or wealth-consumption ratios. For example, if the solution for an operator,Rt, is finite, then we can make a statement on the existence problem forSt.

1.2 Uniqueness for recursive preferences

In this section, we consider the question of the uniqueness of solutions for Epstein-Zin and Ju-Miao utility, which are the main specifications considered in the applied asset pricing models.3The limit solution(2)is a natural choice for models with these preferences in that it can be regarded as the limit of utility from a finite-horizon economy. Despite being a sensible choice, at the level of generality ofEquation (1)it is not the only possible solution. For homogeneous utility specifications such as Epstein-Zin or Ju-Miao, we can show that the limit solution has the key expected properties for utility, and that it is the unique solution to do so. One simple property is that the limit solution itself is homogeneous in consumption, which allows us to formulate the normalized utility that we use in applying our existence results. This allows us to use dynamic programming techniques to rewrite utility and the stochastic discount factor in terms of wealth using the value function (Epstein and Zin 1989;Ju and Miao 2012). This value function satisfies an additional property from dynamic programming by giving the unique homogeneous solution to the Bellman equation, where the agent does not have a rational bubble in his or her optimal portfolio.

To state a precise result requires additional notation that we will only use in this section and the corresponding proofs in Appendix A.3 . Suppose an agent has preferences regarding a random positive consumption stream, C t , given by a recursive utility specification α α U t = C t α α + H t U t + 1 , (7) where H t is an operator that is homogeneous of degree 1 in its argument. 4 If α α > 0 ⁠ , then U t is positive, while if α α < 0 ⁠ , U t is negative. We can rewrite Equation (7) in terms of (1) by defining α α S t x t + 1 = C t α α + H t x t + 1 . (8)

If we take the limit solution for this recursion we get a well-defined utility function on consumption when it converges. The finite-horizon solutions have a straightforward interpretation. If we restrict utility to consumption up to timeT, we get exactly the finite-horizon solution as the value of utility, and the infinite-horizon solution is the limit of these values asT→∞⁠.

The system ofequations (8)can have additional solutions. In particular, nonhomogeneous solutions (seeEquation (A.8)in the appendix) can be present that break the tight link between utility and wealth, allowing us to rewrite the stochastic discount factor in terms of wealth. Even if we restrict the analysis to homogeneous solutions, there still can be exotic solutions (seeEquation (A.9)), where the agents have preferences regarding the asymptotic properties of the consumption stream. As a consequence, agents with these exotic utilities will trade off consumption today for asymptotic properties of the consumption stream at infinity. We show that we can rule out both types of solutions by using properties of the value function. We expect that the value function itself is homogeneous in wealth (which rules out the first type of extraneous solution), and that optimal consumption and wealth under dynamic programming does not have a bubble (which rules out the second type).

Consider the agent’s portfolio choice problem. At time t , the agent chooses a portfolio, which gives wealth W t . There exists an exogenous stochastic discount factor, m t , t + 1 ⁠ , and the agent’s consumption and wealth must satisfy the budget constraint W t − C t = E t ( m t , t + 1 W t + 1 ) .

We say that the agent’s wealth portfolio does not contain a bubble if the agent’s choice of C t and W t satisfies τ τ τ W t = ∑ τ = t ∞ E t ( m t , τ C τ ) ; otherwise it contains a bubble. In this setting, we can prove a relatively strong uniqueness result about the value function for the portfolio problem. Suppose we are in a setting where the value function from utility given by the limit solution is finite. (For example, in any of our applications to the representative agent model in the rest of the paper where we show convergence.) Then let J t ( W t ) be the value function, that is, the maximum attainable utility for W t . This satisfies the Bellman equation for this problem, α α J t ( W t ) = max C t , W t + 1 { C t α α + H t J t + 1 ( W t + 1 ) } . (9)

This recursion can have multiple solutions, just likeEquation (7).

Theorem 3. The recursion (9) has a solution J t that is homogeneous of degree α in wealth. For α α > 0 ⁠ , J t is the smallest homogeneous (of degree α ) positive solution to the Bellman equation, while for α α < 0 ⁠ , J t is the largest homogeneous (of degree α ) negative solution. For any other finite solution, the optimal choice of wealth contains a bubble. Proof. See Appendix A.3 . ■

It also follows from the arguments inAppendix A.3that the value function can be computed by value-function iteration starting from zero. In summary, the Minkowski bound and the Jensen bound from Section 1 enable us to prove the existence of solutions to the general recursion(1). This system, however, can have additional solutions, but these can be ruled out from the properties of the associated portfolio problem. If there is another homogeneous solution toEquation (7), then there is no value function (because the portfolio problem does not have an optimal solution), the value function is infinite, or the optimal solution under dynamic programming has a bubble. The uniqueness results apply to Epstein-Zin and Ju-Miao utility, which are the main applications in this paper.

Next, we will show how Theorem 2 can be applied to derive useful bounds for Epstein-Zin preferences. For this purpose, we compare S t (see Equation (13) ) with the same operator for the special case of expected utility (i.e., for θ = 1): δ α R t x t + 1 = 1 + δ E t ( e α Δ c t + 1 x t + 1 ) . (15)

If we apply Theorem 2 to these two operators, we obtain the following corollary. (SeeAppendix A.2for details.)

Corollary 2 (Jensen Bound for Epstein-Zin Preferences). Let C t be a discrete-time positive stochastic process, that is, C t > 0 ⁠ , and let the agent have the Epstein-Zin preferences given by Equation (10) . Let α 1 > α ≠ 0 be given. If there exists a solution for CRRA utility, θ = 1, then there also exists a solution for Epstein-Zin utility with θ 0 ≠ θ < 1 ⁠ . If there does not exist a solution for CRRA utility, θ = 1, then there also exists no solution for Epstein-Zin utility with θ θ > 1 ⁠ .

The corollary implies that we can use results on the existence of a solution for CRRA utility to obtain insights about existence for Epstein-Zin preferences. So the two existence theorems lead to two bounds for the wealth-consumption ratio. We distinguish between two cases that can be directly characterized by the preference parameters of the agent. The first case is given byθθ<1⁠. In this case, the Minkowski bound provides a lower bound for the wealth-consumption ratio, while the Jensen bound provides an upper bound. Thus, if the Minkowski bound is infinite, the model does not have a solution, while if the Jensen bound is finite, a solution exists. Forθθ>1the roles of the bounds reverse. The Minkowski bound becomes the upper bound, and the Jensen bound becomes the lower bound. Thus, if the Jensen bound diverges, the wealth-consumption ratio diverges, and if the Minkowski bound converges, then the model has a finite solution. In Section 2 we show that for reasonable calibrations of well-known asset pricing models the discrepancy between the bounds is small so that the two bounds are sufficient to settle the existence question for the standard calibrations in the literature. In the next section we show how to derive analytical expressions for the two bounds for a large class of models.

1.4 Discrete-time affine processes

We identify a simple class of models that allow for closed-form expressions for the bounds in asset pricing applications of Theorem 1, namely, when the underlying consumption growth process is a discrete-time affine process. Interestingly, a simple variation of this closed-form solution yields an analytic solution for the special case of CRRA preferences and can hence be used to apply Theorem 2. This makes the application of the two theorems particularly straightforward. In applications, most specifications of exogenous processes fall into the class of discrete-time affine processes; seeEraker (2008)for an introduction. Prominent models with such processes include models with long-run risks and stochastic volatility as in the work ofBansal and Yaron (2004)andSchorfheide, Song, and Yaron (2018), models with stochastic volatility and jumps as in the work ofDrechsler and Yaron (2011), models with volatility of volatility as in the work ofBollerslev, Tauchen, and Zhou (2009),Tauchen (2011), andBollerslev, Xu, and Zhou (2015), and models with time-varying consumption disasters as in the work ofWachter (2013).

1.4.1 Existence bounds for affine state processes

Assume that the state vector X t = ( X 0 , t , X 1 , t , … ) ′ follows a discrete-time affine process. article.2 Existence for Several Asset Pricing Mo:
In this section we derive our existence conditions for different sources of risk that have been shown to be important for asset prices. We begin with long-run risk type models as in the work ofBansal and Yaron (2004)that include both a time-varying mean growth rate and stochastic volatility. We consider three different modeling choices for the variance shocks, all of which guarantee the nonnegativity of the variance process: i.i.d. gamma shocks, for example, as used byLochstoer and Muir (2022)andBansal and Shaliastovich (2013); truncated normal shocks, as considered byde Groot (2015); and Poisson jumps, followingEraker (2008)andDrechsler and Yaron (2011). Second, we consider models with rare consumption disasters as byWachter (2013)andSeo and Wachter (2018). Disaster intensities follow a continuous-time square-root process, which we assume the investor observes at discrete points in time. This ensures nonnegativity of the intensity process and the same approach is used byEraker (2008)to ensure nonnegativity of the variance process.7

All the models we consider share the assumptionsψγψ,γ>1together with a preference for the early resolution of risksγψ(γ>1ψ)in order to explain asset price dynamics. article.3 Learning and Ambiguity Aversion:
In this section we show how to apply our approach to prove existence results for models with additional features, such as regime switching, learning, and ambiguity aversion. Learning, by itself, introduces additional complications to the existence problem (seeGeweke 2001;Pesaran, Pettenuzzo, and Timmermann 2007). Even if asset prices are finite for each possible choice of parameter, the mere prospect of higher growth rates can cause asset prices to become infinite. Mathematically, if the set of possible models is not bounded above, then the integral over individual models can be infinite. Once we allow for learning, smooth ambiguity-aversion preferences (as in the paper byKlibanoff, Marinacci, and Mukerji 2009) fit comfortably into our framework. This also can be combined with timing preferences, as in the paper ofHayashi and Miao (2011). Smooth ambiguity-aversion preferences introduce an additional complication because agents face model uncertainty. They must maintain a probability distribution over the possible models, which they update over time, and they must incorporate this process into their decision-making. Learning coincides with the special case i which agents are ambiguity neutral. In Sections 3.1 and 3.2 we consider two models with smooth ambiguity aversion. In Section 3.3 we outline how to handle other forms of ambiguity aversion.

For smooth ambiguity aversion we state our results in terms of the formulation by Ju and Miao (2012) , which nests Epstein-Zin utility. Agents consider a range of models, parametrized by a parameter π . In each state of the world, s t , the agents have a probability distribution over models. They update the distributions using Bayesian updating. In an Epstein-Zin model with learning, the agents weigh the models by their posterior distribution in that state. If they are also smooth-ambiguity averse, then they have an additional nonlinear transformation. The family of preferences under consideration is given by δ ψ δ γ π η γ ψ η ψ V t = [ ( 1 − δ ) C t 1 − 1 ψ + δ [ E ( ( E ( V t + 1 1 − γ | s t , π ) ) 1 − η 1 − γ | s t ) ] 1 − 1 ψ 1 − η ] 1 1 − 1 ψ , (22) where η γ 1 ≠ η > 0 , 1 ≠ γ > 0 ⁠ , and ψ 1 ≠ ψ > 0 ⁠ . In this parametrization, C t is consumption, δ is the time discount factor, γ determines the level of relative-risk aversion, ψ is the elasticity of intertemporal substitution (EIS), and η is the ambiguity-aversion parameter. The investor has a preference for the early resolution of risks if and only if γ ψ γ > 1 ψ ⁠ . Furthermore, the agent displays ambiguity aversion if and only if η γ η > γ ⁠ . 3.3 Other forms of ambiguity aversion

The original form of ambiguity aversion is the multiple priors model ( Gilboa and Schmeidler 1989 ). Agents have a set of priors, parametrized by Θ. 11 Agent utility is given by β inf Θ E ( ∑ i = 0 ∞ β t u ( C t ) | Θ ) .

If u ( x ) > 0, then this has a very simple bound β β 0 ≤ inf Θ E ( ∑ i = 0 ∞ β t u ( C t ) | Θ ) ≤ E ( ∑ i = 0 ∞ β t u ( C t ) | Θ ) , for any given Θ. Thus, if there exists any Θ, for which it is finite, we have existence. Assume, for example, that u is CRRA utility so that α α u ( C t ) = C t α α ⁠ . If α α > 0 ⁠ , then we can apply the results of Section 1.4 to show existence for any consumption growth process in the discrete affine process class. For negative utility, such as CRRA with α α < 0 ⁠ , we can use the upper bound to prove nonexistence, if for any Θ existence fails. Again, if the consumption growth process is in the discrete affine class we can use the techniques in Section 1.4.

The same technique can be applied to penalized forms of ambiguity aversion, such as multiplier preferences ( Hansen and Sargent 2001 ) or more generally variational preferences ( Maccheroni, Marinacci, and Rustichini 2006 ). In this form, variational utility is given by β inf Θ { E ( ∑ i = 0 ∞ β t u ( C t ) | Θ ) + c ( Θ ) } , where c is a nonnegative convex function of Θ. For multiplier preferences, c is the Kullback-Leibler divergence relative to a baseline model. Note that if c is finite for the set of Θ, then for nonnegative utility it is again sufficient to find any Θ such that expected utility is finite. In particular, for multiplier preferences finiteness holds for typical parametric families of models. Likewise, negative utility nonexistence is easy to show if existence fails for any Θ.
article.4 Conclusion:
This paper presents a novel approach to analyzing existence—that is, whether or not lifetime utility is well defined—and uniqueness for asset pricing models with recursive preferences. For this purpose, we derive a lower and an upper bound for lifetime utility that can be used to prove existence and nonexistence, respectively. To apply our existence theorems, we require only weak conditions in the underlying consumption process, and the theorems hold for a large class of preferences, such as Epstein-Zin utility, smooth ambiguity-aversion, and multiplier preferences; as a result, our approach covers many recent asset pricing models.

Our existence and uniqueness results have several attractive properties that make their application particularly simple. First, the conditions for nonexistence and existence are given by the limit of a simple recursive equation. This limit can be computed in closed form for discrete-time affine processes as well as for models with regime-switching dynamics and learning, and hence the bounds allow for a clear economic interpretation in terms of preference parameters and parameters driving the exogenous consumption process. Second, our existence results pick a specific, unique solution as the limit of finite-horizon solutions. This solution is also the smallest nonnegative solution and it can be viewed as the result of value-function iteration starting at zero. Third, our approach can handle unbounded state spaces. And fourth, it covers non-Gaussian shocks such as jumps and skewed shocks to, for example, volatility. The approach therefore can be used to settle the question of existence and uniqueness for a large number of prominent asset pricing models.

In particular, we consider models with long-run consumption risks and stochastic volatility as found in the work ofBansal and Yaron (2004), models with jumps as found in the work ofDrechsler and Yaron (2011)andWachter (2013), and models with learning and smooth ambiguity-aversion preferences as in the work ofJu and Miao (2012). For these applications, we derive closed-form solutions for the existence conditions in the respective models and show that for the particular calibrations used in the abovementioned papers lifetime utility is well defined. We also prove the claim ofCollin-Dufresne, Johannes, and Lochstoer (2016)that replacing the unit EIS in their first model by a value larger than 1 results in a model for which no well-defined solution exists. Possible directions for future research include extending our approach for proving existence and uniqueness to production economies as inKaltenbrunner and Lochstoer (2010)andCroce (2014), in which case a bound on the endogenous consumption process would be required, to economies with heterogeneous agents as inPohl, Schmedders, and Wilms (2021)or to the existence of prices for risky assets other than the wealth-consumption ratio.

A Proofs for Section 1

In this section we provide proofs for Theorems 1–4. In these proofs, we use a technical condition called the Monotone Convergence Property (MCP). Let S t be an operator that takes time- ( t + 1 ) random variables to time- t random variables. The operator S t satisfies the MCP if for any monotone increasing or decreasing sequence of positive random variables, x i , S t lim i → ∞ x i = lim i → ∞ S t x i . (A.1)

Note that all comparisons are state by state:xt≤ytifωωxt(ω)≤yt(ω)for all statesωin the sample space. The limit is also taken state by state.

Standard utility specifications such as Epstein-Zin and Hayashi-Miao preferences satisfy this property. By the Monotone Convergence Theorem from measure theory (Aliprantis and Border 2006), expectationEwith respect to a measure satisfies the MCP, so any transformation with respect to a continuous function also satisfies it (since it preserves limits). We emphasize this statement as a lemma for convenience.

Lemma 2. The S t and H t operators associated with Epstein-Zin utility ( equations (13) and (14) ) and Hayashi-Miao utility ( equations (24) and (26) ) satisfy the MCP.

The MCP condition has appeared before in the economics literature, though generally in a more abstract form. It is a form of order continuity that makes sense in complete lattices or even partially ordered sets with sufficient suprema.Kamihigashi, Reffett, and Yao (2015)apply it to dynamic programming.Becker and Rincon-Zapatero (2017)use it to reformulate the Thompson metric approach ofMarinacci and Montrucchio (2010).Christensen (2022)uses it to settle several cases of the existence problem.

The main significance of the MCP in these papers is that it allows the authors to prove a theorem known as the Kleene fixed-point theorem or Tarski-Kantorovich fixed-point theorem. Our Theorem 2 can be proven via this fixed-point theorem, but we give a direct proof.

The sequence of consumption and wealth, (Ct,Wt), is said to befeasibleif it satisfies the budget constraint.

At timet, suppose the agent has starting wealth ofWt, and makes a utility-maximizing choice of future wealth, and current and future consumption. LetJt(Wt)be the value function, that is, the level of utility obtained by this optimal choice of consumption and wealth. For completeness, we state and prove a simple proposition.

Proposition 8. If U is homogeneous of degree α , then the value function J t ( W t ) is homogeneous of degree α in wealth. Proof. Homogeneity of utility is sufficient to prove that the value function for the portfolio problem is homogeneous in wealth. If ( C t * , W t * ) is an optimal choice of consumption and wealth for starting wealth W 0 , then λ λ ( λ C t * , λ W t * ) is feasible for starting with λ λ W 0 ⁠ . If it is not optimal, then there exists another consumption and wealth stream ( C t ′ , W t ′ ) that is preferred for starting wealth λ λ W 0 ⁠ . But λ λ ( C t ′ / λ , W t ′ / λ ) is then preferred for starting wealth W 0 , which is a contradiction. ■

Lemma 3. Let ( C t , W t ) be a feasible sequence of consumption and wealth. The sum τ τ τ W ¯ t = ∑ τ = t ∞ E t ( m t , τ C τ ) is convergent, such that W ¯ t ≤ W t ⁠ . Proof. The sequence τ τ τ τ τ τ ∑ τ = t T E t ( m t , τ C τ ) ≤ ∑ τ = t T E t ( m t , τ C τ ) + E t ( m t , T + 1 W T + 1 ) = W t . Thus it is an increasing sequence in T bounded above by W t , so it converges pointwise to a limit below W t . ■

Proposition 9. Let ( C t * , W t * ) be an optimal solution to the portfolio problem for the limit solution, and assume that utility is finite. Then τ τ W t * = ∑ τ = t ∞ E t ( m t , τ C t * ) . Proof. The limit solution is monotonic in consumption: if C 0 ≤ C 0 ′ then U 0 ( C 0 , C 1 , … ) ≤ U 0 ( C 0 ′ , C 1 , … ) ⁠ . Let W ¯ t be from the previous lemma. The sequence ( C t , W ¯ t ) is a feasible consumption and wealth stream for initial wealth W ¯ 0 ⁠ , so if we replace C 0 with C 0 ′ = C 0 + W 0 − W ¯ 0 ⁠ , then we obtain a consumption and a wealth stream feasible for W 0 and higher utility. Then J t ( W t ) = B t J t + 1 ( W t + 1 ) . (A.11)

Proposition 10. The infinite-horizon value function, J t , is the limit of the finite-horizon value functions, so J t = lim T → ∞ J t , T . For α α < 0 ⁠ , the limit J t is the largest negative homogeneous solution of degree α to the Bellman equation. A.4 Proof of Theorem 4

We show how to prove existence for discrete-time affine models with CRRA preferences. The transformed value function (12) for CRRA preferences (i.e., θ = 1) at time t is given by δ α v t = 1 + δ E t ( e α Δ c t + 1 v t + 1 ) , (A.13) where Δ c t + 1 = ln C t + 1 C t and v t denotes the wealth-consumption ratio. In the following, we derive a sufficient condition for a finite solution to (A.13) when log consumption growth, Δ c t + 1 ⁠ , follows a discrete-time affine process.

A discrete-time affine process is a vector stochastic process, ( X 1 , t , … , X n , t ) ′ ⁠ , such that the moment-generating function (MGF) is exponential-linear in the X i , t ⁠ ; specifically, E t ( e k 1 X 1 , t + 1 + ⋯ k n X n , t + 1 ) = exp { h 0 ( k 1 , … , k n ) + h 1 ( k 1 , … , k n ) X 1 , t + ⋯ + h n ( k 1 , … k n ) X n , t } (A.14) for fixed constants, k i , and functions, h i : R n → R , i = 1 , 2 , … n ⁠ . We slightly rewrite the equation above to obtain a compact notation and make it applicable to the CRRA setup. Let X 0 , t ≡ 1 and for the vector k = ( k 0 , k 1 , … , k n ) define h ( v ) to be the vector function term h ( k ) = ( k 0 + h 0 ( k 1 , … , k n ) , h 1 ( k 1 , … , k n ) , … , h n ( k 1 , … , k n ) ) .

Let X t = ( X 0 , t , X 1 , t , … , X n , t ) ′ ⁠ . We then obtain E t ( e k · X t + 1 ) = exp { h ( k ) · X t } . Using the same vector notation as in the compact notation for the MGF, we can rewrite the CRRA recursion (A.13) as τ v t = 1 + E t ( e τ · X t + 1 v t + 1 ) , (A.15) where the state variable Δ c t + 1 is the element X 1 , t + 1 and τ δ α τ = [ ln ( δ ) , α , 0 , … ] ⁠ . The existence of a solution to (A.15) now requires the existence of a finite solution, v t , for any t . We posit that the solution for v t in the recursive Equation (A.15) has the form v t = ∑ i = 0 ∞ e A i · X t (A.16) with a sequence of coefficient vectors ( A i ) i ∈ N ⁠ . Substituting the conjectured expression for v t into Equation (A.15) , we obtain the condition τ τ τ τ τ e A 0 · X t + e A 1 · X t + e A 2 · X t + ⋯ = 1 + E t ( e τ · X t + 1 ( e A 0 · X t + 1 + e A 1 · X t + 1 + ⋯ ) ) = 1 + E t ( e ( τ + A 0 ) · X t + 1 ) + E t ( e ( τ + A 1 ) · X t + 1 ) + ⋯ = 1 + e h ( τ + A 0 ) · X t + e h ( τ + A 1 ) · X t + ⋯ .

We can solve this equation by equating exponents one at a time, so τ τ A 0 = 0 , A 1 = h ( A 0 + τ ) , A 2 = h ( A 1 + τ ) , … .

This approach leads us to a simple recursion for the coefficients, τ A i + 1 = h ( A i + τ ) ⁠ , with the starting point A 0 = 0 ⁠ . The extension of the classical ratio test for convergence (also called the Cauchy ratio test or d’Alembert ratio test) to supremum limits states that the infinite series (A.15) is (absolutely) convergent if lim sup i → ∞ e ( A i − A i − 1 ) · X t < 1.

Note that the two expressions forvt, namely, (A.15, A.16), are those of Theorem 4, so along the way, we have proven the theorem.
